items.q: What is the difference between OR and XOR?
items.correct: OR is true when at least one input is true; XOR is true when exactly one input is true.
items.misconception: They are the same gate with different names.
items.random_wrong: OR is for numbers, XOR is for letters.
items.why: Students miss the critical case: when BOTH inputs are 1. OR gives 1, XOR gives 0. XOR is 'exclusive' - excludes the both-true case.

items.q: What is the main advantage of asymmetric encryption?
items.correct: Secure key exchange without sharing a secret in advance.
items.misconception: It is faster than symmetric encryption.
items.why: Students think asymmetric is just 'better'. It's actually SLOWER than symmetric. Its advantage is solving key distribution - the public key can be shared openly without compromising security.

items.q: What happens when 11111111 + 1 is computed in an 8-bit register?
items.correct: Overflow - the result requires 9 bits but only 8 are available.
items.why: Students miss that registers have fixed width. The mathematical result is 9 bits, but the 8-bit register drops the carry-out, producing 00000000 with an overflow flag.
Correct answer: Kernel. “Shell” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which software lets the OS talk to a printer?
items.why: It's Driver. “Compiler” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many users can a single-user OS support at once?
items.why: The real figure is 1 user. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
